annotations.text: The differential diagnosis must therefore encompass congenital thoracic lesions, neoplastic processes that arise in the posterior mediastinum, and rare infectious or inflammatory entities that can mimic a solid mass in this age group.
Extralobar sequestration remains a leading contender.
Neuroblastoma is a top differential, especially given the posterior mediastinal location and imaging profile.

anemia and thrombocytosis could be reactive to chronic inflammation from an occult infection of the sequestrated tissue, but there is no fever, leukocytosis, or elevated inflammatory markers reported.
Anemia may be secondary to chronic blood loss (unlikely), marrow infiltration (neuroblastoma or lymphoma), or chronic inflammation (sequestration infection).
A dedicated CT angiogram or MR angiography could confirm the systemic arterial supply and definitively differentiate EBPS from other posterior mediastinal masses.
